source_paper_title: Square-Root Regret Bounds for Continuous-Time Episodic Markov Decision Processes

Page 14, Section 4 (Main Results), discussion immediately after Theorem 2.

While the bounds on \(K\) are the most important as they inform the convergence rates of learning algorithms, it remains a significant open question whether one can improve the dependence of these bounds on \(S\), \(H\) and \(\lambda_{\max}\) to narrow down the gap between the upper and lower bounds.
title: Improve minimax regret dependence on states and horizon in episodic CTMDPs
background_latex:
Consider an episodic, finite-horizon continuous-time Markov decision process (CTMDP) with finite state space \(\mathcal S\) (\(|\mathcal S|=S\)), finite action space \(\mathcal A\) (\(|\mathcal A|=A\)), and fixed horizon \([0,H]\). When action \(a\in\mathcal A\) is chosen in state \(x\in\mathcal S\), the process stays in \(x\) for an exponentially distributed holding time with rate \(\lambda(x,a)\in[\lambda_{\min},\lambda_{\max}]\), accrues reward at rate \(r(x,a)\in[0,1]\) during the holding time, and then jumps to a next state \(Y\in\mathcal S\) with probability \(p(\cdot\mid x,a)\) (time-homogeneous). The parameters \(p\) and \(\lambda\) are unknown to the learning agent.

A (deterministic Markov) policy \(\pi\) maps a state and remaining time to an action: \(\pi:\mathcal S\times[0,H]\to\mathcal A\). Starting from a fixed initial state \(x_0\), define the (finite-horizon) value function
\[
V^{\pi}(x,t):=\mathbb E_{\pi}\Big[\int_0^{t} r(X(u),A(u))\,du\,\Big|\,X(0)=x\Big],\qquad (x,t)\in\mathcal S\times[0,H],
\]
and the optimal value \(V^*(x,t):=\sup_{\pi} V^{\pi}(x,t)\).

The agent interacts with the same unknown CTMDP over \(K\) independent episodes, each of length \(H\), restarting from \(x_0\) each episode. Let \(\pi_k\) be the policy used in episode \(k\). The (expected) cumulative regret after \(K\) episodes is
\[
\mathrm{Regret}(K):=\mathbb E\Big[\sum_{k=1}^K \big(V^*(x_0,H)-V^{\pi_k}(x_0,H)\big)\Big].
\]
A minimax (worst-case) regret rate studies \(\sup_{M\in\mathcal C}\mathrm{Regret}(K)\), where \(\mathcal C\) is the class of all such CTMDPs with given \((S,A,H,\lambda_{\min},\lambda_{\max})\) and bounded rewards.

In the referenced work, an upper bound of order \(\widetilde O(\sqrt K)\) is proved but with a leading dependence that is worse than the known lower bound in its dependence on \(S\), on \(H\), and on \(\lambda_{\max}\). In particular, their regret upper bound contains an extra factor \(\sqrt S\) (relative to discrete-time tight bounds) and a worse dependence on \(H\), while their lower bound scales on the order of \(H\sqrt{SAK}\) (up to constants and log factors) in a certain parameter regime.
problem_definition_latex:
Determine whether one can design an episodic reinforcement-learning algorithm for the above finite-horizon tabular CTMDP class \(\mathcal C\) whose worst-case expected regret \(\sup_{M\in\mathcal C}\mathrm{Regret}(K)\) improves the known upper bounds by reducing their dependence on \(S\), \(H\), and \(\lambda_{\max}\), thereby narrowing the gap to the known lower bounds (which already show \(\Omega(H\sqrt{SAK})\) scaling in \(K\) and \(A\) up to log factors).

Equivalently: improve the minimax, instance-independent regret upper bound for episodic finite-horizon tabular CTMDPs beyond the existing \(\widetilde O(\sqrt K)\) bound by achieving sharper polynomial dependence on \(S\), \(H\), and \(\lambda_{\max}\) (and ideally matching the lower bound up to logarithmic factors).
latex:
Consider an episodic, finite-horizon continuous-time Markov decision process (CTMDP) with finite state space \(\mathcal S\) (\(|\mathcal S|=S\)), finite action space \(\mathcal A\) (\(|\mathcal A|=A\)), and fixed horizon \([0,H]\). When action \(a\in\mathcal A\) is chosen in state \(x\in\mathcal S\), the process stays in \(x\) for an exponentially distributed holding time with rate \(\lambda(x,a)\in[\lambda_{\min},\lambda_{\max}]\), accrues reward at rate \(r(x,a)\in[0,1]\) during the holding time, and then jumps to a next state \(Y\in\mathcal S\) with probability \(p(\cdot\mid x,a)\) (time-homogeneous). Determine whether one can design an episodic reinforcement-learning algorithm for the above finite-horizon tabular CTMDP class \(\mathcal C\) whose worst-case expected regret \(\sup_{M\in\mathcal C}\mathrm{Regret}(K)\) improves the known upper bounds by reducing their dependence on \(S\), \(H\), and \(\lambda_{\max}\), thereby narrowing the gap to the known lower bounds (which already show \(\Omega(H\sqrt{SAK})\) scaling in \(K\) and \(A\) up to log factors).

location_in_paper: Page 14, Section 4 (Main Results), discussion immediately after Theorem 2.
open_problem_quote: While the bounds on \(K\) are the most important as they inform the convergence rates of learning algorithms, it remains a significant open question whether one can improve the dependence of these bounds on \(S\), \(H\) and \(\lambda_{\max}\) to narrow down the gap between the upper and lower bounds.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The statement is a broad algorithmic upper-bound improvement question rather than a crisp universally quantified conjecture that can be falsified by a single finite instance. Automated search could explore small CTMDPs to guess scaling, but it cannot directly refute or confirm existence of improved algorithms.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can represent candidate exploration bonuses/planning surrogates and score them by empirical regret on simulated CTMDP families, providing a computable fitness signal. However, empirical performance is only a proxy for minimax regret guarantees, so automated search may suggest promising algorithmic designs but cannot certify the improved theoretical dependence without additional proof work.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
No forward-citation evidence was provided beyond the source paper itself, so there is currently no documented post-2023 work (in the supplied citation analysis) that closes the stated minimax gap for episodic finite-horizon tabular CTMDPs. The source paper establishes the first \,\(\widetilde O(\sqrt K)\) worst-case regret guarantee for learning both transition probabilities \,\(p\) and holding-time rates \,\(\lambda\) under horizon truncation, via CT-UCBVI and a value-iteration-based planning oracle. Its upper bound scales essentially like \,\(\widetilde O(\lambda_{\max}^2 H^2 (H+1/\lambda_{\min})\sqrt{SAK}(\sqrt S+\lambda_{\max}H))\), while its lower bound adapts discrete-time hard instances to show \,\(\Omega(H\sqrt{SAK})\) in a regime where the depth \,\(d\) fits within the expected number of jumps \,\(\lambda_{\max}H\). The remaining gap is thus polynomial in \,\(S\), \,\(H\), and \,\(\lambda_{\max}\), with an explicit extra \,\(\sqrt S\) factor traced to using \,\(\ell_1\) concentration for the full \,\(S\)-dimensional transition vector.

The most promising direction, already suggested by analogy with discrete-time results, is to replace vector \,\(\ell_1\) confidence sets with value-function-dependent (variance-adaptive) confidence bounds, as in Bernstein-style UCBVI analyses, to avoid paying \,\(\sqrt S\) and to sharpen the \,\(H\)-dependence. However, the continuous-time setting introduces two obstacles: (i) the number of decision epochs per episode is random and unbounded, and (ii) horizon truncation means some visits to \,\((x,a)\) do not yield an observed next state, breaking standard counting (pigeonhole) arguments. Any improvement likely requires new concentration/optional-stopping arguments that control the cumulative effect of transition-estimation error weighted by remaining time \,\((H-t)^+\), and a refined coupling between time-spent statistics \,\(T_k(x,a)\) and observed jump counts \,\(N_{k,+}(x,a)\). At present, based on the provided materials, the problem remains open.

sources.title: Square-root regret bounds for continuous-time episodic Markov decision processes
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces CT-UCBVI for episodic finite-horizon tabular CTMDPs with unknown transition probabilities and holding-time rates. Proves worst-case regret upper bound \,\(\widetilde O(\lambda_{\max}^2 H^2 (H+1/\lambda_{\min})\sqrt{SAK}(\sqrt S+\lambda_{\max}H))\) (under a particular parameter choice) and a minimax lower bound of order \,\(\Omega(H\sqrt{SAK})\) in a regime where the depth parameter \,\(d\lesssim \lambda_{\max}H\). Highlights an extra \,\(\sqrt S\) and worse \,\(H\)-dependence in the upper bound relative to discrete-time tight results.
sources.connection: This is the source paper posing the open problem: close the gap between its upper bound and the lower bound by improving polynomial dependence on \,\(S\), \,\(H\), and \,\(\lambda_{\max}\), ideally achieving \,\(\widetilde O(H\sqrt{SAK})\) up to logs.

sources.title: Minimax regret bounds for reinforcement learning
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops UCBVI with Bernstein-style bonuses for episodic finite-horizon tabular discrete-time MDPs, achieving near-minimax regret \,\(\widetilde O(H\sqrt{SAK})\) (plus lower-order terms) under stationary transitions, matching known lower bounds up to logs in appropriate regimes.
sources.connection: Provides the discrete-time benchmark technique (variance-adaptive bonuses and value-function-based confidence) that the CTMDP paper explicitly cites as the missing ingredient to remove the extra \,\(\sqrt S\) and improve \,\(H\)-dependence in continuous time.

sources.title: Episodic reinforcement learning in finite MDPs: Minimax lower bounds revisited
sources.theorem_or_result: Establishes minimax regret lower bounds for episodic finite-horizon tabular discrete-time MDPs, including \,\(\Omega(H\sqrt{SAK})\) scaling (up to logs) in relevant regimes, via hard instance constructions based on A-ary trees.
sources.connection: The CTMDP lower bound in the source paper adapts this discrete-time construction; it anchors what ‘optimal’ dependence on \,\(S\), \,\(A\), \,\(H\) should look like and motivates closing the gap in continuous time.

sources.title: Near-optimal regret bounds for reinforcement learning
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces UCRL2 for unknown MDPs with optimism over confidence sets and proves \,\(\widetilde O(D S\sqrt{AT})\)-type regret bounds in communicating MDPs (average reward), establishing core tools (confidence sets, extended value iteration) used widely in regret analyses.
sources.connection: CT-UCBVI borrows concentration tools (e.g., L1 confidence for transitions) and optimism methodology; improvements for CTMDPs may require sharper confidence/variance tools beyond the L1 vector bound that introduces the extra \,\(\sqrt S\).

sources.title: Exploration-exploitation in MDPs with options
sources.theorem_or_result: Studies exploration-exploitation for semi-Markov decision processes (SMDPs) with options in an infinite-horizon setting, adapting UCRL2-style optimism and obtaining \,\(\widetilde O(\sqrt n)\) regret after \,\(n\) decision steps.
sources.connection: Provides prior regret analysis for continuous-time/semi-Markov models but in infinite-horizon and step-based regret; techniques may inspire handling random holding times, though the episodic finite-horizon truncation issues in CTMDPs remain distinct.

sources.title: Logarithmic regret bounds for continuous-time average-reward markov decision processes
sources.theorem_or_result: Derives instance-dependent (problem-dependent) logarithmic regret bounds for learning tabular CTMDPs in the infinite-horizon average-reward setting, leveraging continuous-time structure and stability/ergodicity conditions.
sources.connection: Shows that sharper (instance-dependent) rates are possible in continuous time, but does not address the minimax episodic finite-horizon dependence on \,\(S\), \,\(H\), \,\(\lambda_{\max}\); techniques might inform refined confidence bounds for rates/transition estimation.
